text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY -- CLINIC FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
SERVICE: GU Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Gerald Hutchinson is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC, BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant, AR-amplified by somatic NGS) presenting today for a scheduled 9-month follow-up on Xtandi. He reports new onset of mid-back pain and worsening fatigue over the past 4-6 weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Hutchinson was originally diagnosed four years ago with high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer (cT3bN1M0, Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, PSA 87.4 ng/mL) and was treated with combined ADT plus abi/prednisone and definitive IMRT (78 Gy to the prostate and seminal vesicles with concurrent pelvic nodal coverage). He achieved a PSA nadir of 0.2 ng/mL. Approximately 12 months ago he was found to have biochemical castration resistance with a PSA doubling time of roughly 3.5 months while on ADT plus abi; confirmatory castrate testosterone at that time was 9 ng/dL. Germline testing revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT), and somatic Foundation One CDx testing on re-biopsy at CRPC transition demonstrated biallelic BRCA2 loss, AR amplification (copy number 8x), TP53 R175H, RB1 deep deletion, PTEN homozygous deletion, and elevated gLOH at 18%. PSMA PET/CT performed at time of CRPC showed a PSMA-avid right iliac chain node and a single focus of uptake at the right posterior iliac crest suspicious for early osseous metastasis not detected on conventional bone scan.

Given prior Zytiga exposure, AR amplification without a known resistance point mutation (no T878A, no F877L), and a desire to hold Lynparza in reserve given concerns about tolerability and optimal sequencing, enza 160 mg orally daily was initiated approximately 9 months ago with continuation of leup for castrate maintenance. abi was discontinued at that time.

His early course on enza was encouraging. At the 3-month mark, PSA had declined to 0.9 ng/mL (61% reduction from 2.3 ng/mL baseline), and he reported only grade 1 fatigue and mild cognitive fogginess without falls or seizures. Restaging bone scan and CT at 6 months showed stable appearance of the known right posterior iliac crest lesion, no new bone lesions, and no visceral metastases; PSA at that time was 0.7 ng/mL, his Xtandi nadir.

Since that nadir, however, PSA has been rising. Serial values over the past approximately 3 months read as follows: 0.7 ng/mL (nadir, at 6-month mark), 1.4 ng/mL (7.5 months), and now 2.8 ng/mL at today's visit. PSA doubling time calculated from these three data points is in the range of 5-6 weeks, which is rapid.

Additionally, Mr. Hutchinson notes new onset of dull, aching mid-back pain, rated 3/10 at rest and worsening to 5-6/10 with prolonged standing or walking. He has been self-managing with acetaminophen with partial relief. He denies radicular symptoms, lower extremity weakness or numbness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or saddle anesthesia. He reports worsening fatigue over the past month, which he describes as a heavier, more pervasive tiredness compared to his prior fatigue on enza. He has also noted mild decreased appetite but no significant weight loss. No fevers, chills, or night sweats.

His hypertension and type 2 diabetes remain managed with his primary care provider. He remains functionally independent at home, living with his wife, though he notes difficulty with some household tasks due to fatigue.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: mid-back pain (grade 1-2), fatigue (grade 2), decreased appetite. Negative: no headache, no visual changes, no chest pain, no dyspnea, no cough, no abdominal pain, no nausea/vomiting, no lower extremity weakness or sensory deficit, no bowel or urinary incontinence, no hematuria, no lower extremity edema, no falls, no seizures, no cognitive changes beyond mild pre-existing fogginess.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 179 lbs (stable from prior visit), Temperature 98.3 F.

General: Well-appearing older gentleman, in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Slightly tired-appearing.

Head/Neck: No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular chains bilaterally.

Chest/Cardiovascular: Heart regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No gynecomastia noted.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender to palpation. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated. No palpable abdominal masses.

Back/Musculoskeletal: Point tenderness on percussion over approximately T9-T11 vertebral bodies, most pronounced at T10. No costovertebral angle tenderness. No tenderness over lumbar spine or sacroiliac joints at today's exam.

Neurological: Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral lower extremities (hip flexion, knee extension, ankle dorsiflexion, great toe extension). Sensation intact to light touch in lower extremities bilaterally. Gait steady without assistive device. Reflexes intact and symmetric.

Genitourinary/Rectal: Deferred today (prior exam stable; prostate without palpable nodularity).

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness.

ECOG Performance Status: 1-2 (reduced from PS 1 at 3-month visit, primarily due to pain and fatigue).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (CURRENT VISIT)
PSA: 2.8 ng/mL (prior: 1.4 ng/mL approximately 6 weeks ago, 0.7 ng/mL nadir at 6-month mark)
Testosterone: 10 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
CBC: WBC 6.1 K/uL, Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Platelets 204 K/uL
CMP: Na 139, K 4.0, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, Glucose 118, Total Bilirubin 0.7, Alkaline Phosphatase 148 U/L (mildly elevated, up from 112 at last visit), ALT 32, AST 28
Alkaline phosphatase trend noted -- prior values: 98 (at Xtandi start), 112 (6-month mark), 148 (today). Mild upward trajectory.
LDH: 218 U/L (mildly elevated)

---

IMAGING REVIEW
Most recent restaging CT and bone scan (6 months into enza therapy): stable single right posterior iliac crest lesion, stable right iliac chain node (under 1.2 cm), no visceral metastases. No new lesions detected at that time. That scan predates today's visit by approximately 3 months.

---

ASSESSMENT
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), BRCA2 biallelic loss, AR-amplified -- PSA and clinical progression on enza. PSA has doubled from nadir in the past 6-7 weeks, with PSA doubling time estimated at roughly 5-6 weeks. The PSA kinetics, combined with new vertebral pain (mid-back, T10 region), point tenderness at T10 on exam, rising alkaline phosphatase, and modestly elevated LDH, raise strong concern for radiographic progression with new or worsening bone metastases. This meets biochemical progression criteria by PCWG3 standards (confirmed rising PSA above nadir, castrate testosterone confirmed).

2. New back pain with vertebral tenderness at T10 -- while there are no neurological findings today (strength and sensation intact, gait normal), the vertebral tenderness in the setting of known metastatic prostate cancer and rapidly rising PSA requires urgent characterization to exclude cord compromise or evolving pathological fracture.

3. Castration-resistant, germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant -- given established biallelic BRCA2 loss confirmed on somatic NGS, the patient has a well-validated actionable alteration for PARP inhibitor therapy (ola per PROfound, Rubraca per TRITON2). This alteration was identified at CRPC transition and enza was used as a bridging strategy. With Xtandi failure, escalation to ola is now the primary next-line consideration.

4. Comorbidities: hypertension and type 2 diabetes are stable. No significant organ dysfunction at this time beyond borderline eGFR (~68 mL/min).

---

PLAN
1. Urgent restaging imaging ordered today: whole-body bone scan (Tc-99m MDP) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Given symptom urgency and concern for spinal involvement, cross-sectional spinal imaging (MRI thoracic and lumbar spine) will also be ordered to assess for cord compromise at T10 and any other vertebral metastases.

2. enza: continued at current dose pending imaging results. Will reassess continuation versus discontinuation based on restaging findings -- if radiographic progression confirmed, plan is to discontinue enza. Xtandi resistance in the setting of prior abi is expected given cross-resistance patterns in the AR pathway.

3. Given imaging results, the plan will almost certainly include initiation of Lynparza 300 mg orally twice daily (based on BRCA2 biallelic loss, FDA indication in mCRPC). Baseline labs for PARP inhibitor initiation will be reviewed at that time (CBC, CMP, urinalysis).

4. If bone progression confirmed with significant spinal disease, radiation oncology consultation will be placed for consideration of palliative EBRT to symptomatic lesions (e.g., T10 if confirmed metastatic with pain).

5. Pain management: Continue scheduled acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours. Consider adding scheduled NSAID coverage if no contraindication from renal function (creatinine 1.1, borderline). If pain escalates or imaging confirms pathological fracture risk, orthopedic spine surgery consultation may be warranted.

6. Bone-targeted therapy: Given new/progressive bone metastases expected, initiate denosumab 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks once progression confirmed. Ensure dental clearance and supplemental calcium/vitamin D.

7. PSA to be repeated at next visit in 2-3 weeks or sooner if clinically indicated. Next clinic appointment scheduled urgently following imaging review.

8. Genetic counseling: already engaged; cascade testing for family members under ongoing management.

Discussed today with Mr. Hutchinson and his wife the concerning PSA trajectory and new back pain. Explained the need for urgent imaging and potential treatment change. Patient is anxious but engaged and understands the plan. He expressed his preference to avoid chemotherapy if alternatives are available, which has been noted and will factor into subsequent therapy discussions.

Time spent: 45 minutes, more than half of which in counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist, GU Medical Oncology]
